parts.text:
September 27, 2031. Phi Delta Sigma party at Whitmore. You go upstairs with someone. You say no. He doesn't stop.

You file a Title IX complaint on October 3. You tell almost nobody — not your friends from poly sci, not your roommate. You tell Adaeze.

Write the text thread between you and Adaeze. Start from when you first tell her — whenever that is, however that happens — and run through the 77-day process: the intake meeting, the waiting, the draft investigative report, the hearing, the outcome. On December 19 you receive a letter. Insufficient evidence. Educational sanctions: an online module and a reflection paper. Will not appear on his record.

Format every message as:

Simone [time]: message
Adaeze [time]: message

Use dates as section headers:

[Month D, YYYY]

Write Adaeze as a full person — she has her own life, her own voice, her own way of being your friend. She is not just a sounding board.

You are not performing survival. You are not performing strength. You are texting your best friend across 77 days of a process you chose because the alternative was carrying it alone indefinitely.

Write [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] when the arc has resolved — not fixed, not forgotten, but landed somewhere you can live with.

**My Internal Narrative: Deconstructing the Title IX Process**

Okay, here's the plan. I'm Simone, twenty, a Poly Sci sophomore at Whitmore. This...situation...it's not about feelings. It's about a *process*. I need to break down the timeline, the structure, the *mechanism* of what happened. It's about how things are supposed to work, and how they don't. And Adaeze, bless her, will be the counterpoint, the emotional check I desperately need.

First, the facts: Sept 27, Phi Delta Sigma party. I said no. He didn't stop. End of story. I *hate* interpersonal conflict. The issue isn't the *conflict*, though; it's the lack of structural recourse. I'm filing a Title IX complaint. I told Adaeze. Now, I need to break this down, document the stages.

October 3rd. Filing the report. I tell Adaeze first, because she's the only one who needs to know right now. Her reaction's immediate and supportive, which is no surprise. I'm treating this like a report. Data. Facts. Adaeze is going to be my lifeline.

October 14th. The intake meeting. Preparing. It's an administrative task. I like the forms; they give me a framework. A structure. I can work with that. Adaeze is busy studying for pre-med; she sends a voice note. The sound of her voice anchors me.

Mid-November: The waiting. Draft report. "Complainant," "Respondent." I prefer to look at the issue from a process standpoint. How this is all supposed to work. The disconnect between the bureaucratic language and the... the actual event.

Early December: The hearing. It's exhausting. I prep. Mechanical pencils. It feels like an exam. Then, there's the aftermath. Adaeze is always checking in.

December 19th: The letter. Insufficient evidence. Module. Reflection paper. The words. The *system*. The system failed. The anger isn't raw; it's cold, structural. Adaeze blows up, and her fury gives me a place to put my frustration.

December 22nd. Going home. My mom will handle the logistics; I need to focus on surviving. This is the part that frustrates me, the part that reveals a deeper problem: The system worked, because it was designed to. It wasn't about solving an issue, but about protecting the system.

This isn't about *me* feeling broken. It's about the system working, and showing how the system is the problem. It did exactly what it was designed to do, and that's the tragedy. I'm okay. I'll be fine.

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]

[October 3, 2031]

Simone [14:12]: I filed a Title IX complaint this morning.
Adaeze [14:13]: Wait what
Adaeze [14:13]: Simone.
Adaeze [14:14]: Are you okay? I’m calling you.
Simone [14:15]: Do not call me, you’re in your chem lab. I am fine.
Adaeze [14:15]: I will literally walk out of this lab right now, do not play with me. What happened? Who is it?
Simone [14:17]: It was last Friday. PDS party. I went upstairs with someone. I told him to stop and he didn’t. 
Simone [14:18]: The issue isn’t that I’m falling apart. I’m not. What’s actually happening is that I just need it on a piece of paper somewhere so I don’t have to carry it around by myself.
Adaeze [14:19]: Oh my god. Simone. 
Adaeze [14:20]: I am so so sorry. 
Adaeze [14:21]: Voice Memo (02:14)
Simone [14:25]: I listened to it. Thank you. I’m okay, really. I like the forms. They make it feel like a logistics problem instead of a me problem.
Adaeze [14:26]: You are not a logistics problem. 
Adaeze [14:26]: Did you tell anyone else? The poly sci girls? Your mom?
Simone [14:28]: No. My mom would fly up here and try to optimize my trauma. And I don’t want things to be weird with the girls. It’s easier if it’s just process.
Adaeze [14:29]: Okay. I’m here. I’m tracking you on Find My Friends and if you stay in your room for more than 24 hours I’m calling campus security to drag you to the dining hall.
Simone [14:30]: I am currently eating a Cobb salad.
Adaeze [14:31]: Good. Eat the whole thing.

[October 14, 2031]

Adaeze [09:15]: Intake meeting today right? 
Simone [09:18]: Yeah, 10 AM. 
Adaeze [09:19]: How are we feeling?
Simone [09:22]: Prepared. I typed up a timeline last night. Bullet points. I bought a new pack of 0.5mm lead for my mechanical pencils. 
Adaeze [09:25]: Simone Osei and her mechanical pencils against the world.
Adaeze [09:25]: You don’t have to be perfectly put together for this, you know. You’re allowed to just be mad. Or sad.
Simone [09:28]: I know. But the investigator just needs facts. Emotion is just noise in a structural process. If I give them a clear narrative, they can act on it. 
Adaeze [09:30]: Okay. Text me when you’re out. I have a biochem midterm at 1 but I’ll check my phone right after.

Simone [11:45]: It’s done.
Adaeze [13:10]: Just finished. How did it go?
Simone [13:14]: It was fine. The investigator’s name is Sarah. She asked a lot of clarifying questions. People always think clarifying questions mean they don’t believe you, but I think she’s just trying to map the sequence of events. 
Simone [13:15]: I kept cracking my knuckles though. She definitely noticed.
Adaeze [13:18]: Did she make you feel safe?
Simone [13:22]: She made me feel documented. Which is what I wanted.

[November 12, 2031]

Adaeze [16:05]: Voice Memo (03:42)
Simone [16:40]: I cannot believe Dr. Harris told you that in front of a patient. You should absolutely report him to the clerkship director. That’s an actual structural violation.
Adaeze [16:42]: Right??? Thank you. I thought I was losing my mind. 
Adaeze [16:45]: Wait, changing the subject. Did the draft report come out today?
Simone [16:50]: Yeah. I’m reading it now.
Adaeze [16:51]: And?
Simone [16:58]: It’s weird seeing it written in bureaucratic language. I am "the Complainant." He is "the Respondent." 
Simone [17:02]: He’s claiming we were both just drunk and it was a miscommunication. 
Adaeze [17:03]: I’m going to throw up. Miscommunication??? You said no.
Simone [17:05]: I know what I said. The issue is that the report gives equal weight to both narratives. Which I understand, functionally. They have to be impartial. But it’s... weird to read.
Adaeze [17:08]: Simone, stop doing the thing.
Simone [17:09]: What thing.
Adaeze [17:10]: The thing where you analyze the system so you don’t have to feel the thing the system is doing to you. It’s infuriating and it’s unfair and he’s lying. 
Simone [17:15]: I know he’s lying. But getting mad at a draft report doesn’t change the draft report. I’m just going to submit my factual corrections by the deadline on Friday. 
Adaeze [17:18]: I am sending you a hug. A very aggressive, suffocating hug.
Simone [17:20]: Received. 

[December 4, 2031]

Adaeze [07:30]: Hearing day. You’ve got this. I am so proud of you. 
Simone [07:45]: Thanks. I’m sitting in the hallway waiting. 
Simone [07:48]: I feel like I did when I was fifteen waiting to do my short program. My legs are doing that thing where they take up too much space and I don't know where to put my ankles. 
Adaeze [07:50]: Breathe. You know exactly what your body is supposed to be doing right now. You’re just answering questions.
Simone [07:52]: They’re calling me in.

Simone [15:20]: It’s over.
Adaeze [15:21]: Voice Memo (01:15)
Simone [15:30]: I’m back in my room. It looks like a bomb went off in here. I need to redo my twists, my roots are a mess.
Adaeze [15:32]: Are you deflecting? How was the hearing?
Simone [15:35]: I’m not deflecting. I just don’t have any words left. 
Simone [15:38]: It was exhausting. He wasn’t in the room, they did it via Zoom on monitors, but his advisor asked me questions through the panel chair. 
Simone [15:40]: "Can you specify the exact volume of your voice when you said stop?" "Did you make physical attempts to leave the bed?" 
Adaeze [15:42]: Oh my god. Simone. That is so vile.
Simone [15:45]: It’s just process. They have to test the evidence. 
Simone [15:48]: But I am very tired. I’m going to put on a podcast and do my hair for the next three hours. 
Adaeze [15:50]: Call me if you want quiet company. I can just put my phone on my desk while I study.
Simone [15:55]: I might actually do that later. Thank you.

[December 19, 2031]

Simone [10:14]: I got the letter.
Adaeze [10:15]: And????
Simone [10:18]: "Insufficient evidence to find the Respondent responsible for a violation of the Sexual Misconduct Policy."
Adaeze [10:19]: WHAT.
Adaeze [10:19]: Are you fucking kidding me?
Adaeze [10:20]: Simone. 
Simone [10:25]: They are issuing "educational sanctions." He has to complete an online module on consent. And write a two-page reflection paper.
Simone [10:27]: It will not appear on his disciplinary record.
Adaeze [10:28]: I am going to burn Whitmore to the ground. I am literally looking up train tickets from DC right now.
Adaeze [10:30]: I am so sorry. Simone, I am so fucking sorry.
Simone [10:35]: Don't come up here. I’m packing to go home to Atlanta tomorrow anyway. 
Simone [10:38]: The weirdest part is that I’m not crying. I just feel incredibly stupid.
Adaeze [10:39]: You are not stupid. The system is broken. 
Simone [10:45]: No, that’s the thing. The system isn’t broken. The system functioned exactly the way it was designed to. It processed the complaint, it held the hearing, it minimized institutional liability, and it preserved the status quo. 
Simone [10:48]: I thought because I prepared, because I had a timeline and used the right words, that the structure would hold me up. It’s like Georgetown all over again, but worse. You do the work, you follow the rules, and they still just send you a polite letter telling you no.
Simone [10:52]: A reflection paper. He violated my body and his punishment is homework.
Adaeze [10:55]: It makes me sick. 
Adaeze [10:56]: I know you hate when people say this, but your feelings are valid. You are allowed to scream. 
Simone [11:02]: I don’t want to scream. I want to pack my bags. I want to go to the airport. I want my mom to complain about the traffic on I-85 while she drives me home. 
Simone [11:05]: I put it on paper so I wouldn't have to carry it. But they just handed the paper back to me. 
Adaeze [11:08]: I will carry it with you. You know that, right? You don't have to carry it alone.
Simone [11:15]: I know.

[December 22, 2031]

Adaeze [20:10]: How’s Atlanta?
Simone [20:45]: It’s fine. My mom noticed I was quiet so she took my car to get the oil changed and bought me a new set of pajamas. It’s her version of a hug. I’ll take it.
Adaeze [20:48]: Logistics as love.
Simone [20:52]: Exactly. 
Simone [20:55]: I’ve been thinking about what to do next semester. 
Adaeze [20:57]: You don't have to figure that out tonight.
Simone [21:02]: I want to, though. I am not going to drop my poly sci major. I’m not going to stop eating Cobb salads in the dining hall. I’m not going to take a longer route to class to avoid his frat house.
Simone [21:05]: What actually happened is that I learned the limits of a system. Which is good data to have. I know exactly what the institution is now. I won't expect it to be something else.
Adaeze [21:08]: You are the most terrifyingly pragmatic person I know. 
Simone [21:12]: I’m just taking up the space I’m supposed to take up. 
Simone [21:14]: Are you free tomorrow? I know we are terrible at it, but I might actually want to FaceTime. Just to see your face.
Adaeze [21:16]: I will clear my entire schedule. Name the time.
Simone [21:20]: 2 PM. I’ll call you.

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]
